title: Falls Prevention for Families: What to Do If Your Parent Is at Risk
description: Plain language guide for families managing fall risk in aging parents. Warning signs, home safety, and getting professional assessment.

blocks.question: What should I do if I'm worried my parent might fall?
blocks.directAnswer: Start by recognizing the warning signs. Recent falls, balance problems, weakness, or confusion raise risk. Talk to your parent's doctor about falls screening. Ask about home hazards. Small changes like better lighting, handrails, and removing clutter prevent most falls. A professional assessment can identify exactly what your parent needs.

tldrBullets.text: 1 in 4 older adults falls each year. Most falls are preventable with simple changes.
Warning signs include recent falls, poor balance, weakness, confusion, or vision problems.
Home modifications, exercise, and professional assessment are proven to reduce fall risk.

blocks.expandedAnswer:
If you're worried about your parent falling, you're not alone. Falls are common in older age but not inevitable. Most falls happen at home and are preventable with practical changes.

Your parent might not tell you they've fallen or nearly fallen. They may fear losing independence or worrying the family. Ask directly: 'Have you had any falls or near misses recently?' Listen to their concerns about balance, weakness, or fear of falling. These feelings are real and worth taking seriously.

Falls often have multiple causes. Poor lighting, weak legs, confusion, medication side effects, and home hazards all play a role. Your job isn't to make your parent immobile and safe. It's to understand the risks and make practical changes that preserve independence while reducing injury risk.

sources.title: National Council on Aging: Falls Prevention
National Institute on Aging: Prevent Falls and Fractures

blocks.body:
Warning signs that your parent might be at risk

Watch for recent falls, even if your parent says they're fine. A single fall is a warning. Multiple near-misses (stumbling, catching themselves, feeling dizzy) are also important. Ask: 'Do you feel unsteady? Do you worry about falling? Have you changed how you move around the house?'

Other signs include sudden weakness in the legs, difficulty standing from a chair, shuffling gait, confusion or memory problems, and vision changes. If your parent has had a stroke, broken bones, or other health events recently, fall risk is higher.

Medication matters. Some medicines increase dizziness or confusion. If your parent started a new medicine before falls started, mention this to their doctor. Not all medicines are the culprit, but timing is worth exploring.

What to do first: Conversations with your parent and doctor

Start a conversation without being alarmist. 'I've noticed you seem a bit wobbly when you stand. I'm wondering if we can talk to your doctor about a falls check.' Frame it as helpful, not controlling.

Book an appointment with your parent's doctor and mention falls or fall risk. Ask for a falls screening assessment. The doctor can check medication, balance, strength, and vision. If your parent has fallen recently, the doctor should offer an urgent assessment.

During the appointment, mention any warning signs you've noticed. Tell the doctor about recent events: hospital stays, new medicines, changes at home. If your parent is anxious about falling, the doctor should take this seriously. Fear of falling is itself a risk factor, and treatment can help.

Ask the doctor about a referral to a physical therapist or occupational therapist. These professionals can assess your parent at home and recommend specific exercises or modifications. This service may be covered by Medicare or your parent's insurance.

Simple home modifications that prevent falls

You don't need expensive renovations. Small changes matter most. Start with lighting: ensure hallways, stairs, and bathrooms are well lit. Add a nightlight between the bedroom and toilet. Good lighting helps your parent see hazards.

Remove tripping hazards. Loose rugs, clutter, and cables cause falls. Encourage your parent to clear pathways. Keep frequently used items at waist height so they don't bend or reach dangerously.

Stairs need attention. Handrails on both sides are essential. Ensure stairs are clear and well lit. Some families install stair lifts or restrict stair use. Ask your parent about this preference.

Bathrooms are high-risk. Install grab bars in the shower and next to the toilet. Use non-slip mats. A shower chair or seat reduces balance demands. Check that the toilet seat isn't too low (a raised seat helps).

Footwear matters. Slippers without grip or high heels increase falls. Encourage non-slip indoor shoes. Proper outdoor footwear with good grip is important if your parent goes outside.

Getting a professional assessment

A formal falls assessment by a physical therapist or occupational therapist is valuable. They visit your parent's home, observe how they move, test balance and strength, and identify specific hazards. They recommend targeted exercises and practical modifications.

Assessment results help you understand your parent's specific risks. One parent might need balance training. Another might need medication review. A third might benefit from assistive devices like a walker or cane. Personalized recommendations are more effective than generic advice.

Professional assessment also documents your parent's baseline. If falls increase later, you have a record of previous status. This is helpful for doctor follow-up and identifying changes over time.

Able Assess is a tablet-based falls screening tool that helps professionals assess your parent systematically. It captures information about mobility, balance, home environment, and medications. Results guide recommendations and track progress if your parent starts exercise or home modifications.

stats.stat: 1 in 4 older adults experiences at least one fall per year
Most falls happen at home and are preventable with simple modifications
Home-based exercise and environmental modifications reduce falls by 30-40%

items.question: My parent had a fall but they say they're fine. Should I still worry?
items.answer: Yes. Even a single fall is a warning sign. Falls often follow a pattern: one fall increases the risk of another. Your parent might minimize the fall out of fear or embarrassment. Encourage them to tell their doctor even if they feel okay. A screening assessment is worth doing.

items.question: How can I persuade my parent to make home changes without making them feel controlled?
items.answer: Involve your parent in decisions. Walk through the home together and ask what concerns them. Listen to their priorities. If they're worried about stairs, address that first. If they resist changes, explain the benefit (safety) without implying they can't manage. Frame it as helping them stay independent.

items.question: Is a walker or cane a sign of decline?
items.answer: No. Assistive devices help people stay active and independent. A walker or cane reduces balance demands and allows your parent to do more, not less. Many people delay using one due to stigma, but this increases fall risk. If a professional recommends an aid, it's usually a good idea.

items.question: What if my parent refuses help or won't see a doctor?
items.answer: This is frustrating. Try a softer approach: 'I've been reading about falls in older adults and I'm worried. Could we just talk to the doctor to rule out anything serious?' Sometimes framing it as a health check rather than a fall risk issue reduces resistance.

items.question: Is exercise safe for someone at high fall risk?
items.answer: Yes. Exercise reduces fall risk by improving balance, strength, and confidence. But it should be supervised by a physical therapist initially to ensure it's safe and targets the right areas. Your parent's doctor or occupational therapist can arrange this.

blocks.heading: Get a professional assessment for your parent
blocks.bodyText: A falls screening assessment identifies what your parent needs: whether it's home modifications, exercises, or other support. Able Assess helps professionals assess fall risk systematically.
